executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

National Medicaid enrollment has reached 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with most major states experiencing enrollment declines following the end of pandemic-era continuous coverage protections. The largest states show year-over-year decreases ranging from 1.8% in New York to 7.6% in Michigan, with Texas posting a moderate 3.1% decline to 4.0 million enrollees. Notably, North Carolina stands as the only major state with positive growth at 0.5%, suggesting effective retention strategies worth examining. Total program expenditures reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with significant per-enrollee cost variations across states—ranging from Florida's efficient $2,031 per enrollee to Pennsylvania's high-cost $4,377 per enrollee. Texas maintains a reasonable $2,988 per-enrollee cost, positioning it below the national average of $2,869 despite serving a large, diverse population.

Quality performance remains a critical concern, with national scores averaging 65.6 out of 100 and substantial state variation. High-performing states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80) demonstrate the potential for excellence, while bottom-tier states including Mississippi (48) and Oklahoma (51) require immediate intervention. Texas ranks 30th nationally with a score of 62, placing it in the moderate performance tier but below states with similar demographics and managed care penetration rates. Given Texas's robust 93.3% managed care penetration—well above the 83.2% national average—there appears to be untapped potential for quality improvements through enhanced managed care organization accountability and performance incentives. Leadership should prioritize quality improvement initiatives and consider adopting best practices from top-performing states while leveraging the state's strong managed care infrastructure to drive better outcomes.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees as of January 2026, representing a manageable -3.1% year-over-year decline that aligns closely with national unwinding trends and matches Pennsylvania's reduction rate. This enrollment decrease is notably more controlled than several peer states, including Michigan (-7.6%) and Illinois (-6.2%), though North Carolina stands out among southern states with slight growth (+0.5%). At $2,988 per enrollee, Texas maintains spending discipline significantly below the national average of $2,869—though this figure appears inconsistent and requires verification—while keeping total expenditures at $12.0 billion. The state's managed care penetration of 93.3% substantially exceeds the national average of 83.2%, demonstrating strong commitment to coordinated care delivery and cost management through private plan partnerships.

However, Texas faces persistent quality challenges that warrant immediate attention. With a quality score of 62/100, ranking 30th nationally, the state falls in the "moderate" performance tier but lags behind regional peers and remains closer to bottom-performing southern states like Arkansas (54) than top performers. This quality gap is particularly concerning given Texas's non-expansion status, which likely contributes to coverage gaps and care coordination challenges for low-income adults. The state's relatively low per-enrollee spending, while fiscally prudent, may reflect restricted eligibility and benefit limitations rather than efficiency gains. Key areas to monitor include whether the high managed care penetration will translate into measurable quality improvements, how ongoing federal regulatory changes around HCBS quality measures will impact Texas's performance scores, and whether the state can maintain enrollment stability while improving care outcomes for its substantial Medicaid population.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania's Per-Enrollee Spending Significantly Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is 53% higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost efficiency issues.

alerts.title: Multiple Southern States Show Critical Quality Performance
alerts.description: Five states (MS, OK, AL, SC, AR) have quality scores below 55, with Mississippi at critically low 48 out of 100.

alerts.title: Florida's Unusually Low Per-Enrollee Spending Raises Questions
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average despite being the 4th largest program by enrollment.

alerts.title: Massachusetts Excels in Both Quality and Spending Efficiency
alerts.description: Massachusetts leads with 81 quality score while maintaining reasonable per-enrollee spending of $4,201, demonstrating high-value care delivery.

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score lags at 62/100, ranking #30 nationally
executiveInsights.summary: Texas scored 62/100 on quality measures, placing it in the moderate tier and ranking 30th out of 50 states. This is significantly below the national average of 65.6 and well below high-performing states like MA (81) and MN (80).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts contract renewals and federal oversight scrutiny. Poor quality scores can trigger enhanced federal monitoring and affect our positioning for future opportunities.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement initiatives to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs 4% above national average at $2,988
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869, representing a $119 premium per member. With 4M+ enrollees, this translates to approximately $480M in excess costs annually.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs indicate potential inefficiencies that HHSC will want to address. This creates opportunities for cost optimization consulting but also suggests budget pressure.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost drivers and prepare efficiency improvement proposals

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declined 3.1% YoY to 4.03M members
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% year-over-year to 4,032,864 members, consistent with the national trend of declining enrollment post-pandemic. This matches the decline rate of PA but is better than IL (-6.2%) and MI (-7.6%).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines affect revenue streams and may trigger contract adjustments or renegotiations. Understanding the drivers is critical for forecasting and resource planning.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment trend impacts on current contract terms

executiveInsights.title: New CMS HCBS quality measures for 2028 published in Federal Register
executiveInsights.summary: CMS published new Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set requirements for 2028. This represents upcoming regulatory changes that will affect quality reporting and potentially contract requirements.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New quality measures will require system updates and process changes. Early preparation gives Accenture competitive advantage in helping HHSC comply and potentially improve their quality ranking.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review 2028 HCBS quality requirements for compliance gaps

executiveInsights.title: Texas achieves 93.3% managed care penetration, above national 83.2%
executiveInsights.summary: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86M managed care enrollees, significantly higher than the national average of 83.2%. This indicates strong managed care infrastructure and coordination capabilities.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration suggests Texas is well-positioned for value-based care initiatives and population health management, creating opportunities for advanced analytics and care coordination services.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Explore value-based care expansion opportunities with HHSC

riskOpportunity.label: EVV system renewal
riskOpportunity.description: Texas HHSC Electronic Visit Verification system contract approaching renewal with potential for modernization expansion.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Proactively engage HHSC on EVV modernization roadmap and position Accenture's digital health capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: High managed care penetration at 93.3% creates risk of MCO contract disruptions but opportunity for systems integration work.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor MCO contract cycles and develop partnership strategies with leading health plans.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 nationally in quality scores, creating pressure for performance improvement initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement strategy targeting Texas's specific performance gaps.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight escalation
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio oversight could trigger compliance reviews or audits affecting current contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring and documentation processes across all Texas HHSC engagements.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline impact
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment declining 3.1% YoY may reduce program funding and administrative contract values.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop value-based contracting models that maintain revenue despite enrollment fluctuations.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS modernization mandate
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Medicaid HCBS quality measure requirements will drive significant system and process changes.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position Accenture as lead implementation partner for HCBS quality measurement system development.

riskOpportunity.label: Cost efficiency pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas per-enrollee costs above national average at $2,988 vs $2,869 may trigger cost reduction mandates.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive cost optimization strategy focusing on administrative efficiency and fraud prevention.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid enrollment decreased 3.1% year-over-year to 4,032,864 enrollees.

pulseMetrics.label: 12-Mo Enrollment Delta
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas enrollment declined 3.1% compared to national average decline of approximately 4.2%.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee, slightly above the national average of $2,869.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas managed care penetration of 93.3% exceeds the national average of 83.2%.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas scored 62 out of 100 on quality measures, ranking 30th nationally in moderate tier.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active Texas procurement opportunities identified: HHSC portal and ESBD search portal.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Six active OIG and regulatory signals identified affecting Medicaid operations.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific milestone dates provided in current data.
